channel.title: Cross-Functional on Athan Dial - Portfolio
channel.description: Recent content in Cross-Functional on Athan Dial - Portfolio

item.title: Learning Agendas: Bringing Research Rigor to Product Decisions
item.description:
Context

By 2025, Montai ran multiple concurrent R&D experiments — AI model iterations, assay validations, Anthrolog generation improvements. Each experiment had implicit goals but lacked explicit success criteria. The result: debates about “when to pivot” and “when to scale” became opinion-driven rather than evidence-backed.

Facts:

  • By 2025: Multiple concurrent experiments (AI models, assays, analog generation improvements)
  • Problem: Unclear success criteria per experiment (when to pivot? when to scale?)
  • Example confusion: “AI model improved accuracy” but didn’t translate to better compound selection
  • Stakes: Wasted months on meandering experiments without clear learning goals

The core issue traced back to a fundamental principle from my PhD training: experiments without pre-defined hypotheses produce data, not learning. In academic research, you write your aims before running experiments. In biotech R&D, we were running experiments and retroactively deciding whether results “felt good enough.” This had to change.

item.title: Operating Discipline in Compound Nominations
item.description:
Context

Each nomination wave locked $30K–$100K of procurement and months of wet-lab capacity. A sloppy compound picklist translated directly into sunk experimental runs with no way to learn from the outcome.

The company’s computational drug discovery group could produce ranked compound lists on demand, yet every screening cycle restarted from zero. Scientists ran their own filters, mixed vendor catalogs with local enumerations, and argued from memory because knowledge lived in a system or in someone’s head. Assay readouts landed six months later with no mechanism to attribute success or failure back to the initial decision. The consequence: we could not learn whether structural diversity constraints mattered, whether AI rankings were overfitting, or whether procurement dollars were allocated to the right exploration vs. exploitation balance.
